coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of carbon quantum dots via optimized hydrothermal treatment using cellulose nanocrystals as precursors

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are receiving increasing attention due to their tunable redox activity, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent aqueous dispersion. However, their low quantum yield remains a significant impediment to their synthesis and practical application. In the present work, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were utilized as precursors for the optimized hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Subsequently, the synthesized CQDs were electrodeposited onto a carbon-coated surface. The influence of hydrothermal temperature and time on the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of CQDs was systematically explored. The successfully synthesized CQDs exhibited an average particle size of approximately 5 nm. The quantum yield was enhanced from 14.35 % to 23.7 % as the hydrothermal temperature increased from 180 °C to 230 °C. Additionally, the electrochemical properties of the composite films were investigated. Electrochemical assessments demonstrated an increase in specific capacitance from 60.4 mF·cm−2 to 65.2 mF·cm−2 with an elevation in temperature from 180 °C to 210 °C. Remarkably, the CQDs displayed higher energy density (8.819 mWh·cm−2) and power density (1800 mW·cm−2) at 210 °C for 8 h. This work offers a scalable approach for the efficient production of high-performance CQDs, showcasing their substantial potential for supercapacitor applications.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of carbon quantum dots via optimized hydrothermal treatment using cellulose nanocrystals as precursors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are receiving increasing attention due to their tunable redox activity, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent aqueous dispersion. However, their low quantum yield remains a significant impediment to their synthesis and practical application. In the present work, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were utilized as precursors for the optimized hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Subsequently, the synthesized CQDs were electrodeposited onto a carbon-coated surface. The influence of hydrothermal temperature and time on the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of CQDs was systematically explored. The successfully synthesized CQDs exhibited an average particle size of approximately 5 nm. The quantum yield was enhanced from 14.35 % to 23.7 % as the hydrothermal temperature increased from 180 °C to 230 °C. Additionally, the electrochemical properties of the composite films were investigated. Electrochemical assessments demonstrated an increase in specific capacitance from 60.4 mF·cm−2 to 65.2 mF·cm−2 with an elevation in temperature from 180 °C to 210 °C. Remarkably, the CQDs displayed higher energy density (8.819 mWh·cm−2) and power density (1800 mW·cm−2) at 210 °C for 8 h. This work offers a scalable approach for the efficient production of high-performance CQDs, showcasing their substantial potential for supercapacitor applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted much attention due to their tunable redox activity, abundant surface functional groups, excellent aqueous dispersion, low production cost, and wide range of raw materials [1–3]. CQD synthesis techniques are categorized into two approaches, namely top-down and bottom-up strategies [4–6]. The top-down approach includes methods such as laser ablation, chemical oxidation, chemical exfoliation, arc-discharge, and ultrasonication [4,5]. For instance, Sunita Dey et al. [7] successfully prepared graphene quantum dots with a quantum yield of 2.5 %, which was generated through arc discharge in a gas phase environment. Hu et al. [8], on the other hand, utilized laser irradiation of graphite flakes suspended in a polymer solution to synthesize CQDs with a quantum yield of 12.2 %. However, employing top-down techniques comes with many limitations, such as high energy consumption, the necessity for complex equipment, and difficulties in mass production. In contrast, hydrothermal synthesis stands out for its simple and cost-effective advantages in the bottom-up strategy [9,10]. Additionally, hydrothermal synthesis facilitates the formation of oxygen-containing surface functional groups, thereby imparting water-soluble properties and excellent dispersibility [11]. For example, Li et al. [12] also reported the synthesis of CQDs with varying quantum yield, especially 4.26 % for green CQDs using hydrothermal treatment. Given the pivotal role played by the quantum yield in economic viability, there is considerable significance in the improvement of the quantum yield of CQDs through optimized hydrothermal treatments.

In addition to the processing method, the selection of raw materials also has a considerable influence on the quantum yield. Typically, a variety of substances, including citric acid, urea, o-phenylenediamine, milk, orange juice, and lignocellulose, can readily undergo dehydration, polymerization, aromatization, and condensation reactions to synthesize CQDs [9,13,14]. The production of CQDs from lignocellulose has received considerable attention in recent years due to its abundance as a renewable resource and the potential to possess excellent properties with a range of promising applications [4,15]. Zhao et al. [16] reported that the main hydrothermal degradation products of lignocellulose, serving as precursors, were used to simulate and explore the synthesis of CQDs. Jiang et al. [6] prepared N-doped hemicellulose CQDs with a relatively high yield by employing a deep eutectic solvent composed of choline chloride and urea for hemicellulose separation. Consequently, CQDs synthesized from biomass materials exhibit unique advantages. Remarkably, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are of significant research interest given their abundance, low-cost, and renewable nature with a high degree of aliphatic and aromatic hydroxyl groups, which facilitate the condensation and carbonation processes, ultimately leading to the formation of CQDs [4,15,17,18]. Hence, CNCs offer distinct advantages as raw materials for CQD preparation and represent an essential avenue for low-cost, eco-friendly synthesis.

Indeed, extensive research has previously been conducted by our team on the preparation, modification, and functionalization of CNCs for various applications [19–21]. To further expand the applications of CNCs, enhance their added value, and produce high-performance materials, CQDs were initially synthesized via hydrothermal methods using CNCs as raw materials. Subsequently, these CQDs were deposited on carbon cloth to prepare electrodes. In particular, the influence of hydrothermal temperature and time on both the yield of CQDs and their electrochemical properties, as well as the CQD microstructure, was comprehensively investigated. Ultimately, the CQDs-based films were prepared to study their electrochemical properties in comparison with other reported literature. This work will offer an effective solution for enhancing the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of CQDs via optimized hydrothermal treatment, utilizing CNCs as precursors.

Commercial microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) powders were sourced from Shanghai Tonnor Material Science Co., Ltd., China. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %, AR) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %, AR) were supplied by Hangzhou Shuanglin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Carbon cloth (WOS1009) was provided by Taiwan Carbon Energy Technology Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were used without further purification, and deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

MCC (2.5 g) was placed into a 500 mL round-bottomed flask, followed by the addition of 64 % H2SO4 solution (250 mL). The resulting suspension was stirred with a magnetic stirrer and kept at 50 °C for 2 h duration to achieve suspension samples containing CNCs. Afterward, the suspension was rapidly cooled to ambient temperature with tap water and then centrifuged (TGL-15B centrifuge, Shanghai Anting Scientific Instrument Factory, China.) at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was decanted, and 40 mL of water was added to the resulting precipitate. After mixing with a glass rod, the mixture underwent repeated centrifugation cycles. The centrifugation/washing process was repeated 5 times. Finally, the CNC suspension was concentrated to 2 wt% using a rotary evaporator (N-1001, Tokyo Rikakikai Co., Ltd., Japan).

The CNC suspension (pH = 3.5, measured using a portable pH meter, PHB-5, INESA Co., Ltd. China) was transferred into a hydrothermal synthesis reactor (238A617–01, ASHCROFT Inc., USA.) and maintained at 180 °C by a thermostat (Al, YUDIAN Automation Technology Co., Ltd. China). After a 2 h reaction, the obtained brown-yellow CQD suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the collected supernatant was stored in a brown volumetric flask. According to the hydrothermal temperature and time of CNCs, the supernatant was named CQDs-T-t (T (hydrothermal temperature) = 180 °C, 210 °C, 230 °C, 250 °C; t (hydrothermal time) = 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deposition of CQDs on carbon cloth
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, carbon cloth was treated with H2O2 (30 wt%) at 90 °C for 120 min. The pre-treated carbon cloth was obtained after washing and drying. Then, the CQD films were electrodeposited onto the pre-treated carbon cloth using a three-electrode system (a platinum chip electrode and a saturated calomel electrode serving as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively) with an electrolyte of CQDs. The electrochemical deposition was conducted at a voltage of 2.5 V at room temperature for 40 min. Additionally, the electrodeposited area of pre-treated carbon cloth was 1 cm × 1 cm. After electrodeposition, the resulting CQD-based films were washed with deionized water and then dried. The mass loading of the electrodeposited CQD films onto the pre-treated carbon cloth was approximately 0.1 mg. The preparation process of CQDs and the electrodeposition on carbon cloth are shown in Fig. 1a.

The crystal structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, A8 Advance, BRUKER AXS GMBH., Germany). The FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Nicolet IS50 instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, GeminiSEM 500, Carl Zeiss AG, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL F200, Japan Electronics Co., Ltd., Japan) were employed to observe the microstructures. The uniformity of the CQDs on the surface of the carbon cloth was characterized using Laser microscopic confocal Raman microscopy (CRM, In Via, Renishaw, England). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+, USA) and ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Agilent Cary 60, Agilent Technologies Inc., China) were used to analyze surface elemental components and chemical states. Photoluminescence emission spectra were obtained by a Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (FL, USA) at room temperature.

Using quinine sulfate as a standard sample, the CQDs prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures and times were calculated in quantum yield according to Eq. (1).

Where S and R represented the CQDs solution and quinine sulfate reference solution, respectively; QY was the quantum yield; I was the integral of the intensity of the fluorescence emission spectrum; A was the absorbance of the solution at 330 nm; and η was the refractive index of the solution. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in a 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution.

All electrochemical properties were tested using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Chenhua Instrument Factory, Shanghai, China) with a three-electrode system (a platinum chip electrode and a saturated calomel electrode as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively), and 1.0 M H2SO4 as the electrolyte. The areal specific capacitance (Cp, mF·cm−2) energy density (mWh·cm−2), and power density (mW·cm−2) of the electrode were calculated according to the following equations.

Here, I (A), t (s), ΔV (V), and S (cm2) were the discharging current, the discharging time, the discharging potential range (1 V), and the areal of electrode material, individually. E and P stood for energy density (mWh·cm−2) and power density (mW·cm−2) of the film, respectively.

Hydrothermal temperature and hydrothermal time are important factors affecting the quantum yield of CQDs [22,23]. It is crucial to investigate how the two factors of quantum yield optimize the production process, reduce costs, and enhance the utilization of CNCs. Fluorescence spectra of CQDs prepared by reacting for 6 h at temperatures ranging from 180 to 250 °C and excited at a wavelength of 330 nm, were shown in Fig. 2a. When the temperature was increased from 180 to 210 °C, the quantum yield increased from 14.35 % to 21.56 %. However, the quantum yield decreased to 19.2 % while the temperature was further increased to 250 °C, possibly due to the activation of non-radiative pathways, where more excited electrons returned to the ground state through non-radiative processes [24,25].

The emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm for different hydrothermal times were exhibited in Fig. 2b. Increasing the hydrothermal time from 2 h to 6 h resulted in a notable enhancement in the photoluminescence intensity. This change correlated with the quantum yield results shown in Table 1 , suggesting that the increased degree of carbonization and the formation of a denser and more regular structure contributed to this enhancement [26]. Nevertheless, it was observed that the quantum yield exhibited a slight decline with an increase in hydrothermal time. Zheng [27] similarly found that the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs prepared initially increased and then decreased with extended times, in accordance with the quantum yield trend. This subsequent photoluminescence quenching might be attributed to percolation among these sp2 configurations, facilitating the transport of excitons to non-radiative recombination sites [25].

The quantum yield was detailedly presented in Table S1. Increasing both the hydrothermal time and temperature enhanced the quantum yield of CQDs compared to CQDs-180 °C-2 h. Specifically, the CQDs-230 °C-6 h exhibited a yield of 23.7 % following appropriate hydrothermal treatment, utilizing CNCs as raw material. As outlined in the existing literature, the formation of CQDs can be primarily divided into two stages: the depolymerization of macromolecules and the re-polymerization of small molecules [28]. Among these factors, the aromatic ring and the active site facilitate the polycondensation reaction and the carbonation process. CNCs possess surface oxygen-containing functional groups, as well as a small particle size, which allows for a more thorough reaction [29]. The Ki value represented the sum of the test results corresponding to level i in any given column. The R-value represented the range, which was calculated as the difference between the maximum and minimum values of K. Based on the K and R values, it could be observed that the hydrothermal temperature exerted a more prominent impact compared to time [23,30]. Excessively high hydrothermal temperature and time were detrimental to the synthesis of CQDs.

In summary, it was evident that temperature and time exerted a profound influence on the surface state and degree of carbonization of CQDs, with the optimal hydrothermal condition identified as 230 °C for 6 h [23–26]. Using these conditions, the process for preparing CQDs described in this paper was not only simple and utilized renewable raw materials, but also yielded higher results compared to other literature (Table 1).

To assess practical application efficiency after optimizing quantum yield for production cost-effectiveness, the focus then shifted to evaluating the electrochemical performance. The electrochemical properties of CQDs-based films prepared at different hydrothermal temperatures and times were investigated. The effect of temperature on the electrochemical properties of CQDs-based films was shown in Fig. 3a. It was found that the specific capacitance exhibited an increase from 60.4 mF·cm−2 to 65.2 mF·cm−2 with the increased hydrothermal temperature from 180 °C to 210 °C. However, when the temperature reached 250 °C, the specific capacitance decreased to 6.1 mF·cm−2. The elevated temperatures resulted in the severe carbonization, particle growth and aggregation of CQDs, which might lead to a deterioration in their electrochemical performance. The effect of time on the electrochemical properties of CQDs-based films was exhibited in Fig. 3b. As the hydrothermal time was increased, the specific capacitance gradually rose, reaching its peak at 8 h. To further ascertain that the CQDs exhibit the optimal electrochemical properties at a hydrothermal time of 8 h, an additional hydrolysis experiment was conducted for 10 h. However, no notable enhancement in the electrochemical properties was observed. It could therefore be concluded that CQDs exhibited the optimal electrochemical performance at a hydrothermal temperature of 210 °C and a hydrothermal time of 8 h.

To further investigate the electrochemical properties of the prepared CQDs-based films, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, which had the best electrochemical properties, were selected for detailed analysis. Furthermore, two additional control groups (CQDs-250 °C-8 h and CQDs-210 °C-2 h) were also included for comparison. Fig. 4a showed the cyclic voltammetry of pre-treated carbon cloth, CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h at a scan rate of 1 mA·cm−2. The negligible current response of the pre‑carbon cloth indicated that the significant capacitive activity of the CQDs-based films mainly originated from the electrodeposited CQDs. The capacitance in the rectangular region was primarily attributed to the electrical double-layer capacitor contribution of the CQDs, whereas the pseudo-capacitance stemmed mainly from the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups within the CQDs [11,33]. In CQDs, the π-electron network that occurred from sp2 hybridization might serve as electron acceptors/donors, as a conducting medium for electron transport, or as a bridge for electron transfer. The electrochemical performance of CQDs-210 °C-8 h demonstrated an upward trend with the increase of the scan rate in Fig. 4b. In addition, different capacitive properties could be observed among CQDs prepared at various hydrothermal temperatures and times.

The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of pre-treated carbon cloth, CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h were presented in Fig. 4c. The nearly symmetric curves indicated the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the prepared CQDs-based films [33–36]. The charge/discharge time of CQDs-210 °C-8 h exhibited a reduction as the current density increased (Fig. 4d). At a current density of 1 mA·cm−2, the surface specific capacitance of the CQDs-210 °C-8 h film electrodes reached 65.2 mF·cm−2 (the mass specific capacitance was 658 F·g−1 calculated based on the loading of the CQDs film). In comparison to alternative techniques for the synthesis of CQDs, this study demonstrated superior electrochemical performance [37–39].

To further explore the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance of the electrodes, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was performed on the CQDs-based film electrodes (Fig. 4e). The equivalent circuit was fitted to the EIS by Z-view software and the associated resistances were shown in Table 2 . The EIS results for the carbon cloth and CQDs-based film samples, presented as Nyquist plots in Fig. 4e, displayed a semicircle at the high-frequency region and a straight line at the low-frequency region. The high-frequency region reflected the kinetics of the electrode reaction (charge transfer process), and the low-frequency region was related to the diffusion of reactants (products) [40]. Owing to the coexistence of electrochemical and concentration polarization, the equivalent circuit of the electrochemical system could be simplified and represented as shown in the inset of Fig. 4e. The Nyquist plot implied that CQDs-210 °C-8 h exhibited a smaller RΩ value (0.98 Ω), indicating smaller equivalent series resistance. Furthermore, the Rct was 0.4 Ω, suggesting smaller charge transfer resistance. These results supported the fact that good electrode and electrolyte interactions contributed to the high electrochemical performance of CQDs-210 °C-8 h.

In the low-frequency region, the CQDs-based film electrodes had a small charge transfer resistance, indicating that the ions or electrolytes diffuse more easily to the surface of the electrodes [41,42]. The time constant (τ 0) was a minimal in the Bode phase plot (Fig. 4f), indicating a superior frequency response of CQDs-210 °C-8 h [43]. CQDs synthesized under optimal hydrothermal conditions exhibited promising electrochemical properties [40,44,45].

To investigate the effect of current density and hydrothermal conditions on area-specific capacitance, the area specific capacitance of CQDs was presented in Fig. 5a. Increasing both temperature and time significantly improved the area specific capacitance compared to CQDs-210 °C-2 h. However, prolonged hydrothermal time led to a notable decrease in the area specific capacitance, which may be mainly due to a reduction in the content of oxygen-containing functional groups involved in the reaction [46,47]. To verify the multiplicative performance of the CQDs-based film electrodes, the long-cycle stability of the CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h electrodes was evaluated by repeating the charge-discharge experiments at 1 mA·cm−2 (Fig. 5b). The experimental results demonstrated excellence cycling stability of the CQDs-based film electrodes.

To evaluate the performance of CQDs-based films, Ragone plots were demonstrated in Fig. 5c. Based on the total area of the electrode's active material, the energy density and power density of CQDs-210 °C-8 h reached 8.819 mWh·cm−2 and 500 mW·cm−2, respectively. It preserved 80.3 % of its energy density as the power density increased to 5000 mW·cm−2. These results surpassed those obtained in previous literature reports, such as rGO-Co3O4 nanocomposite (15.1 Wh·cm−2 at 17.79 mW·kg cm−2, Ref. [48]), NV@CNWAs CNT fibers (96.07 μWh·cm−2 at 270 μW·cm−2, Ref. [49]), CQDs-PANI (33.8 μWh·cm−2 at 0.3 mW·cm−2, Ref. [50]), and MWNT/carbon fiber (9.8 μWh·cm−2 at 189.4 μW·cm−2, Ref. [51]). Furthermore, the energy density and power density on the mass of the CQDs-based films were also investigated (Fig. 5d). When the current density increased from 10 to 100 A·g−1, the energy density decreased from 91.39 to 73.38 Wh·kg−1, while the power density increased from 5181.0 to 51,797.6 W·kg−1 for CQDs-210 °C-8 h. These values were superior to those reported in previous studies, such as CQDs/PPy-NW (30.92 Wh·kg−1 at 224 W·kg−1, Ref. [52]), rGO/CQDs-HP (22.4 Wh·kg−1 at 76.68 W·kg−1, Ref. [53]), PPy/CQDs (26.44 Wh·kg−1 at 18090.82 W·kg−1, Ref. [54]) and CQDs-polymer gel (50 Wh·kg−1 at 8534 W·kg−1, Ref. [55]). CQDs-210 °C-8 h were shown to have greater energy density (8.819 mWh·cm−2) and power density (1800 mW·cm−2), highlighting their potential for capacitor applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology of CQDs and CQDs-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of carbon cloth, pre-treated carbon cloth, and CQDs-based films was characterized using FE-SEM. As demonstrated in Fig. 6a, untreated carbon cloth exhibited an extremely smooth surface, primarily due to the application of finishing auxiliaries to avoid the adhesion of dust and other contaminants during the transport and production processes [56]. As compared to Fig. 6a, hydrophilic pre-treated carbon cloth (Fig. 6b) displayed tiny droplet shapes, indicative of improved hydrophilicity [57]. Fig. 6c showed the rod-like structure of CNCs before hydrothermal treatment, while Fig. 6d, e, and f demonstrated the apparent morphology of CQDs electrodeposited on carbon cloth prepared under different hydrothermal temperatures and times. Compared with the pre-treated carbon cloth, the surface of carbon cloth with electrodeposited CQDs appeared rougher and more rugged, with visible CQD particles.

In Fig. 7a, b and c, the typical TEM image of CQDs revealed that each single CQD particle was well-dispersed and uniform. The CQDs exhibited parallel lattice planes with a spacing distance of around 0.2 nm, consistent with the interlayer distance of graphitic (sp2) carbon [23]. Besides, the size distributions of CQDs shown in Fig. 7d, e and f implied that the average sizes of CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h and CQDs-250 °C-8 h were approximately 4.95 nm, 4.92 nm, and 5.28 nm, respectively [58].

The trace in Fig. 8a showed the Raman spectrum of CQDs-210 °C-8 h. Two typical characteristic peaks located at 1341 and 1595 cm−1 were observed, which were attributed to the D- and G-band of carbon, respectively [59]. To assess the distribution of CQDs on the surface of the carbon cloth, CRM mapping was conducted on CQDs-210 °C-8 h. The mapping results of CQDs-210 °C-8 h demonstrated that the peak intensities across the surface of the carbon cloth were approximately equal, thereby confirming the uniform distribution of CQDs on the surface of the carbon cloth [60,61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterization of CQDs and CQDs-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectrum of CNCs exhibited peaks corresponding to specific functional groups (Fig. 9a). The peaks at 3311 and 1670 cm−1 were related to the stretching and bending vibration of OH groups in cellulose, respectively. The peak at 2885 cm−1 was attributed to CH stretching, which was derived from the aliphatic hydrocarbons. The peaks at 1394 cm−1 reflected bending vibrations of CH and CO groups in aromatic rings of polysaccharides, while the peak at 1024 cm−1 indicated C–O–C pyranose ring stretching vibration (anti-symmetric in phase ring). Moreover, a small shoulder at 807 cm−1, unique to CNCs, represented C–O–SO3 group vibration owing to the formation of sulfate esters on the CNC surface during hydrolysis [17,18,62,63]. In contrast to the CNCs, CQDs-based films (CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h) presented several common peaks at 1336 cm−1 (CO), 1641 cm−1, 1164 cm−1, and 1101 cm−1. The FT-IR peaks at 1164 and 1101 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric bridge C–O–C stretching and the asymmetric in-plane ring stretching vibrations, respectively [2,64]. Moreover, besides the vibration band of CQDs, the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration bands of C–O–C also indicated that the surface of CQDs was partially oxidized during the hydrothermal process [3,65,66]. The prominent peak at 1641 cm−1, corresponding to CC stretching vibrations of the aromatic carbon skeleton, clearly indicated the formation of CQDs with a graphene-like aromatic carbon structure through hydrothermal carbonization of CNCs [67].

The optical properties of CQDs were investigated by UV‐vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 9b). The UV spectra showed an absorption peak near 230 nm, which was induced by the π-π* transition potentially occurring in the aromatic CC of CQDs [68]. These findings were consistent with previous reports [30]. It was characterized that the prepared CQDs exhibited green fluorescence when excited at 365 nm, which was a typical feature of CQDs [35,69].

The XRD plot (Fig. 9c) illustrated the variation in peak intensity observed for CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h. The diffractogram of CNCs exhibited three prominent diffraction peaks at around 2θ = 14.9°, 16.4°, and 22.8° in agreement with the crystalline planes of (1
1 ¯
0), (110), and (200), respectively, characteristic of cellulose I [17,18,63,70]. In contrast, the XRD patterns of CQDs displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23.5°, indicating an amorphous carbon phase with abundant defects [23]. The interlayer spacing of the CQDs was calculated as 0.204 nm. In addition, this XRD data was also in good agreement with the measured distance between the two lattice planes observed in TEM. Fig. S2 showed the selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs, indicating that the carbon dots were organized in a partially crystalline manner. The decreased peak intensity of CQDs at 210 °C for 2 h was reduced compared to CNCs, which could be attributed to the hydrothermal process disrupting the crystallinity of CNCs, resulting in an amorphous structure [65].

The zeta potentials of CQDs-210 °C-2 h, CQDs-210 °C-8 h, and CQDs-250 °C-8 h were found to be −4.31 mv, −8.31 mv, and −2.186 mv, respectively (Fig. 9d), indicating that the surface of the CQDs was negatively charged, with CQDs-210 °C-8 h being more abundantly negatively charged.

XPS analysis was conducted to explore the chemical composition, chemical state, and surface distribution of CNC and CQDs samples, as presented in Fig. 10 . The survey scans identified three main elements: C, S, and O (Fig. 10a), with detailed elemental ratios provided in Table 3 . The C 1s spectrum showed four peaks at 284.5, 285.8, 286.8, and 288.9 eV (Fig. 10b), which were ascribed to the CC/CC, CO, CO, and COO bonds, respectively [4,15]. The presence of CC, CO, and OC–OH bonds indicated that the CQDs possessed hydroxyl, epoxy, and carbonyl groups, as also evidenced by FT-IR [23]. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of CQDs in aqueous solutions. The two peaks at 531.6 and 532.7 eV in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 10c) could be attributed to the CO and COH/C–O–C groups [3,23]. Additionally, the peak at 168.9 eV in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 10d) revealed the presence of the –SO3 group. For CNCs, in addition to C (68.9 %) and O (30.04 %), a small amount of S (1.07 %) was also detected. The decrease in the O/C ratio (from 0.32 to 0.19) from CQDs-210 °C-2 h to CQDs-210 °C-8 h was mainly attributed to the increase in carbon content during hydrothermal treatment [30]. Conversely, the increase in the (O/C) ratio (from 0.19 to 0.24) from CQDs-210 °C-8 h to CQDs-250 °C-8 h might indicate the disintegration of CQDs aggregates [11]. Therefore, the surface chemistry can also be tailored by the hydrothermal treatment.

To enhance the quantum yield and electrochemical properties, CQDs were prepared via optimized hydrothermal synthesis using CNCs as precursors. The effect of hydrothermal temperature and time on the quantum yield and electrochemical properties of CQDs was systematically investigated. Firstly, the obtained CQDs were characterized to exhibit typical green fluorescence when excited at 365 nm. It was found that the quantum yield exhibited an increase from 14.35 % to 23.7 % as the hydrothermal temperature was elevated from 180 to 230 °C. Moreover, a positive correlation was found between the quantum yield and the hydrothermal time within the range of 2–6 h. Subsequently, the CQDs were electrodeposited onto the surface of carbon clothes, and the electrochemical performance and microstructure of the films were investigated. Electrochemical measurement indicated that the specific capacitance rose from 60.4 mF·cm−2 to 65.2 mF·cm−2 with the increased temperature from 180 °C to 210 °C. The specific capacitance reached a maximum value at 8 h with an increase from 4 to 10 h. Remarkably, CQDs-210 °C-8 h were shown to have greater energy density (8.819 mWh·cm−2) and power density (1800 mW·cm−2). Overall, this work offers a scalable solution for the efficient preparation of high-performance CQDs, promising potential for applications in supercapacitors.

Ruru Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ya Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Feiyun Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Yaoting Liang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Yanjun Tang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
